title: Recruiting Families Using Data Act of 2025
short_title: Recruiting Families Using Data Act of 2025
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
plain_summary: This bill requires states to make plans for finding and keeping good foster and adoptive families for children who need homes. States must work to place children with family members when possible and find families that match what each child needs. Every year, states have to report to Congress about how many foster and adoptive families they have, what kinds of families they are, and what problems they face in finding good families. The goal is to help more children find stable, loving homes that are right for them.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Recruiting Families Using Data Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  State Plan Amendment
sections.text: This section changes the Social Security Act to require states to create better plans for finding and keeping foster and adoptive families. States must make a family partnership plan that includes several important parts. First, states must work with birth families, relatives, foster families, adoptive families, community groups, and young people who have been in foster care when making this plan. Second, the plan must explain how the state will find relatives and other people connected to children who could take care of them. It must also describe how the state will make special recruitment plans for every child who needs a foster or adoptive family. The plan must show how children and teens will be included in efforts to find them families. States must use information and data to set goals, measure progress, reduce the number of children in group homes, help more children find permanent families, make placements more stable, place more children with relatives, and better recruit families for teenagers and groups of brothers and sisters. The plan must also explain how the state will create or support advisory boards made up of foster families to help improve recruitment and keeping families involved. Every year, states must collect and report information about how many foster families they have, what these families look like, how many group homes are being used, and detailed information about licensed foster families and children in group care both in the state and in other states. States must also update their plans every year with feedback from foster and adoptive parents and young people about licensing, training, support, and reasons why parents stop fostering or why adoptions fail. Each year, states must analyze and report on specific challenges in recruiting families that match the racial and ethnic backgrounds of children in foster care, and describe their efforts to overcome these challenges. The Secretary of Health and Human Services can require other information about recruiting and keeping foster and adoptive families. These changes start on October first, two thousand twenty six. However, if a state needs to pass new laws to meet these requirements, they have until the first day of the first three month period that begins after their state legislature meets for the first time after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 3  Inclusion of Information on Foster and Adoptive Families in Annual Child Welfare Outcomes Report to Congress
sections.text: This section changes the law to require more information in the yearly report that goes to Congress about child welfare outcomes. Starting with the report for fiscal year two thousand twenty five and every year after that, the report must include three new things. First, it must have information for each state about the number, demographics, and characteristics of foster and adoptive families in that state, plus the number of potential families that are not being used and the reasons why. Second, it must summarize the challenges and barriers to being a foster or adoptive parent, including problems with recruitment, licensing, engagement, and retention, and reasons why parents stop fostering, adoptions fail, or families struggle, based on surveys that states conduct with foster and adoptive parents. Third, it must summarize the challenges and barriers that states report when trying to recruit families that match the racial and ethnic backgrounds of children in foster care in their state, and describe efforts to overcome those barriers.
